coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric fluorescent probe with dual near infrared emission for in vivo ratio imaging of cysteine
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Zheng, Bing
Wang, Shulong
Xu, Jiayao
Huang, Lixian
Zhao, Shulin
coredata.dc:description: Accurately detecting cysteine (Cys) in vivo is crucial for diagnosing Cys-related diseases. A novel ratiometric fluorescent probe featuring dual near-infrared emission is developed in this study for the in vivo ratio imaging of Cys. The probe comprises a hemicyanine organic small-molecule dye (HCy-CYS) with specific Cys recognition capabilities covalently coupled with carbon dots (CDs) synthesized using glutathione (GSH) as the carbon source (GCDs), forming a unique composite nanofluorescent probe (GCDs@CYS). The probe undergoes a specific reaction with acrylate upon the addition of Cys, converting HCy-CYS to HCy-OH. Consequently, the GCD fluorescence intensity at 685 nm gradually decreases, whereas that of HCy-OH at 720 nm progressively increases, yielding a ratiometric fluorescence signal. Notably, both emission wavelengths of the probe exceed 650 nm, thereby effectively mitigating the interference from background signals during cellular and in vivo imaging. Furthermore, the probe demonstrates high specificity for Cys, enabling its differentiation from homocysteine and GSH. The Cys concentration and fluorescence ratiometric intensity exhibit a strong linear correlation at 10–150 μM with a detection limit of 0.95 μM. These results indicate that the ratiometric fluorescent probe can serve as a valuable tool for monitoring Cys-related physiological or pathological processes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescent probe
Cysteine
Carbon dots
Near-infrared emission
Ratio imaging

Determination of the Cys content in cell lysates
Ratiometric fluorescence in vivo imaging of Cys
Synthesis and response mechanism of the GCDs@CYS probe for Cys detection
Spectral properties of the GCDs, HCy-OH, and GCDs@CYS
Detection of Cys content in cell lysate
Ratio fluorescence imaging of Cys in HeLa cells
In vivo ratio imaging of Cys

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the GCDs. Inset shows an HRTEM image of the GCDs. (b) Particle size distribution of the GCDs. (c) XPS spectrum of the GCDs. (d) FTIR spectra of the GCDs and GCDs@CYS.

(a) UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation, and emission spectra of the GCDs. (b) UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation, and emission spectra of HCy-OH. (c) Absorption spectrum of HCy-OH and fluorescence emission spectrum of the GCDs. (d) Changes in the fluorescence spectrum of GCDs@CYS before and after adding Cys (PBS solution with a pH of 7.0).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of GCDs@CYS (10 μg/mL) after reacting with different concentrations of Cys in PBS solutions (pH = 7.0). (b) Linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (F720 nm/F685 nm) of GCDs@CYS solutions with different concentrations of Cys. (c) Fluorescence intensity ratio (F720 nm/F685 nm) of GCDs@CYS (10 μg/mL) solutions in the presence of different substances in PBS solutions (pH = 7.0). (d) Viability of cells under different concentrations of GCDs and GCDs@CYS.

Performance of GCDs@CYS for the fluorescence imaging of Cys in HeLa cells. (a) HeLa cells were co-incubated with the GCDs@CYS probe (10 μg/mL). (b) HeLa cells were pretreated with NEM (500 μM) and then co-incubated with the GCDs@CYS probe (10 μg/mL). (c) HeLa cells were first incubated with NEM (500 μM), treated with Cys (200 μM), and finally incubated with the GCDs@CYS probe (10 μg/mL). The green and red channels were respectively collected at 650–700 nm and 700–750 nm under 633 nm excitation. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Time-dependent fluorescence imaging of the Cys levels in mice using the GCDs@CYS probe. (a, b) Mice injected with GCDs@CYS (20 μg/mL). (c, d) Mice treated with NEM (100 μL, 10 mM) for 30 min and then injected with GCDs@CYS (20 μg/mL). The GCDs and W–OH fluorescence were collected using 700 and 750 nm filters, respectively.
Schematic diagram illustrating the GCDs@CYS probe for Cys detection.
Schematic diagram illustrating the reaction mechanism for Cys detection by GCDs@CYS.
Detection of Cys in HeLa cell lysates.

head.ce:title: A ratiometric fluorescent probe with dual near infrared emission for in vivo ratio imaging of cysteine

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Agricultural Resources Chemistry and Biotechnology
Guangxi Colleges and Universities Key Laboratory of Efficient Utilization of Special Resources in Southeast Guangxi
College of Chemistry and Food Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Accurately detecting cysteine (Cys) in vivo is crucial for diagnosing Cys-related diseases. A novel ratiometric fluorescent probe featuring dual near-infrared emission is developed in this study for the in vivo ratio imaging of Cys. The probe comprises a hemicyanine organic small-molecule dye (HCy-CYS) with specific Cys recognition capabilities covalently coupled with carbon dots (CDs) synthesized using glutathione (GSH) as the carbon source (GCDs), forming a unique composite nanofluorescent probe (GCDs@CYS). The probe undergoes a specific reaction with acrylate upon the addition of Cys, converting HCy-CYS to HCy-OH. Consequently, the GCD fluorescence intensity at 685 nm gradually decreases, whereas that of HCy-OH at 720 nm progressively increases, yielding a ratiometric fluorescence signal. Notably, both emission wavelengths of the probe exceed 650 nm, thereby effectively mitigating the interference from background signals during cellular and in vivo imaging. Furthermore, the probe demonstrates high specificity for Cys, enabling its differentiation from homocysteine and GSH. The Cys concentration and fluorescence ratiometric intensity exhibit a strong linear correlation at 10–150 μM with a detection limit of 0.95 μM. These results indicate that the ratiometric fluorescent probe can serve as a valuable tool for monitoring Cys-related physiological or pathological processes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cysteine (Cys), a sulfhydryl-containing small-molecule amino acid, plays a crucial role in many physiological and pathological processes [1]. Cys is the only amino acid among the biological thiols involved in the synthesis of proteins, peptide fragments, enzyme active sites, and cofactors [2,3]. Moreover, Cys is an important biomolecule involved in the regulation of biological nitrogen and redox homeostasis [4‒6]. Excess or a deficiency of Cys is closely associated with diseases such as rheumatoid arthritis, Parkinson's disease, Alzheimer's disease, liver damage, growth retardation, leukocyte loss, and cardiovascular disease [7‒10]. The intracellular levels of Cys range at 30–200 μM, while the concentration of plasma Cys in blood is approximately 250 μM, which is 25-fold higher than the levels of homocysteine (Hcy; 10 μM). Normal levels of Cys are involved in the synthesis of various proteins and the major antioxidant glutathione (GSH) and are a source of sulfides in the human metabolism [11‒13]. Cys is produced in biological systems from methionine (Met) mainly by Met adenosyltransferase, adenosine Hcy lyase, cystathionine-β-synthase, and cystathionine-γ-cleavage synthase. In mammalian cells, Cys dioxygenase catalyzes the conversion of Cys to Cys sulfinic acid, followed by further enzymatic conversion to β-sulfinylpyruvate via aspartate aminotransferase, and then decomposition to pyruvate and SO2, which exists in biological environments as a HSO3 − and SO3 2− equilibrium. Therefore, the detection of Cys in biological systems is important and has received increasing attention [14].

Various methods for detecting Cys have been developed, including high-performance liquid chromatography, mass spectrometry, electrochemical analysis, ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy, and capillary electrophoresis [15‒17]. However, these methods require complex sample pretreatments such as derivatization, protein precipitation, extraction, filtration, and dilution. Fluorescence imaging, as compared with other technologies, is favored by researchers because of its high selectivity, sensitivity, and good biocompatibility. Various organic small-molecule fluorescent probes have been developed to detect Cys through Michael addition, aldehyde cyclization, and mercaptan cracking reactions [18]. However, most synthetic probes are organic small-molecule probes, and their excitation and emission wavelengths are in the short-wavelength region, which is not conducive to cell imaging. Furthermore, distinguishing between Cys and Hcy is challenging because of the similarity of their molecular skeletons and reactivities with those of GSH (such as the SH groups) [19]. Long-wavelength fluorescent probes can avoid cell auto-fluorescence, reduce background interference, and are beneficial in cell and biological imaging [20,21]. Therefore, near-infrared fluorescent molecular probes for specific in vivo Cys detection are highly desirable.

Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted significant attention because of their easy surface modification, excellent water solubility, good biocompatibility, and excellent photostability. CDs have been successfully applied in targeted imaging and drug delivery [22‒27]. Ratiometric fluorescent probes can improve the detection sensitivity through self-referencing and calibration owing to the two emission peaks, effectively avoiding the influence of factors such as the probe concentration, background signal, instrumentation, and temperature [28‒31].

Combining CDs with organic small-molecule probes can yield ratio fluorescent probes for Cys detection. Wang et al. [32] covalently coupled CDs with fluorescein derivatives to prepare the specific fluorescent probe CD-FITA, which exhibited good water solubility, low toxicity, and high specificity for Cys and could be used to detect endogenous Cys in cells. However, the excitation and emission wavelengths were <550 nm, which is unsuitable for in vivo imaging.

A hemicyanine organic small-molecule dye with specific Cys recognition capabilities (HCy-CYS) and CDs synthesized with (GSH) as the carbon source (GCDs) were prepared in this study. HCy-CYS was then covalently coupled with the GCDs to form a novel composite nanofluorescent probe (GCDs@CYS). Acrylate, which has a high specificity for Cys and can distinguish it from Hcy and GSH, was used as the recognition group in HCy-CYS. This probe combines the advantages of the high fluorescence intensity and high specificity of organic small-molecule probes with the low toxicity, high biocompatibility, and good stability of GCDs. The probe exhibits a ratiometric fluorescence response, which can improve the detection accuracy through ratiometric correction. The dual emission wavelength of the probe exceeded 650 nm, effectively avoiding interference from background signals during cell and in vivo imaging. This probe is suitable for Cys imaging detection in cells and in vivo and can accurately visualize in vivo changes in Cys levels in real time.
The Supporting Information provides more detailed information on the chemicals, materials, and synthesis of HCy-OH and HCy-CYS.

GSH (0.6854 g) was added to a high-pressure reactor containing 40 mL formamide. The reactor was then placed in an oven and reacted for 3 h at 160 °C. After the reaction was complete, the reactor was cooled to room temperature. The resulting solution was then centrifuged to separate the components. The supernatant was collected and filtered through a 0.22-μm filter membrane to remove larger particles. Subsequently, a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off of 1000 Da was used to dialyze the supernatant against ultrapure water for one week until the water became colorless. Finally, green GCDs were successfully synthesized.

HCy-CYS (0.0269 g) was dissolved in 1.0 mL DMSO to obtain a 5 mM stock solution. The HCy-CYS stock solution was diluted with water, and EDC (0.1 g) and NHS (0.1 g) were added for activation. After stirring the mixture for 1.0 h, GCDs were added, and the mixture was stirred for an additional 12 h. The resulting GCDs@CYS nanoprobe was obtained by dialyzing the mixture to remove the free HCy-CYS, EDC, and NHS.

The optical properties of the GCDs@CYS nanoprobe were evaluated using UV–vis and fluorescence spectrophotometry. The morphology, surface structure, and particle size of the GCDs and GCDs@CYS were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Absorption and fluorescence spectra of GCDs@CYS
ce:section.ce:para: The absorption and fluorescence spectra of the GCDs@CYS nanoprobe (10 μg/mL) were measured using UV–vis and fluorescence spectrophotometry after reacting with different concentrations of Cys in PBS solutions (pH = 7.0). To investigate the specificity of Cys, other common ions, mercaptans, and amino acids were added under the same conditions, and their spectral data and changes were determined after a 10-min reaction.

The toxicities of the GCDs and GCDs@CYS were determined using the MTT method. After digesting HeLa cells, the cells were inoculated into a 96-well plate and cultured in an incubator (37 °C, 5 % CO2). When the cell density reached approximately 90 %, GCDs and GCDs@CYS were added at different concentrations for a 24h incubation. Subsequently, a 20 μL MTT solution was added to each well, and the incubation was continued for 4 h. The medium was then discarded, 100 μL DMSO was added to each well, and the well plates were shaken for 10 min. The plates were placed in an enzyme labeler to determine the absorbance at 490 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the Cys content in cell lysates
ce:section.ce:para: HeLa cell lysate (20 μL) was added to the GCDs@CYS solution, and the fluorescence spectrum of the resulting solution was measured using an excitation wavelength of 627 nm. Subsequently, 10, 50, and 100 μM of Cys solution were added, and the fluorescence spectra were measured. The Cys content in the HeLa cell lysate and the recovery rate were calculated using the determined fluorescence intensity.

HeLa cells were digested, inoculated in a confocal Petri dish, and cultured in an incubator (37 °C, 5 % CO2). When the cell density reached approximately 60 %, the first group was incubated with the GCDs@CYS nanoprobe (10 μg/mL) for 0.5 h. A second group was prepared by first adding 500 μM N-ethyl maleimide (NEM; a mercaptan scavenger) to the solution and incubating for 0.5 h, followed by washing with PBS thrice, adding the GCDs@CYS nanoprobe (10 μg/mL), and incubating for 0.5 h. A third group was prepared by first adding NEM (500 μM) to the solution and incubating for 0.5 h. The solution was then discarded from the cells, followed by washing with PBS thrice, incubation with Cys (200 μM) for 0.5 h, washing with PBS thrice, and the addition of GCDs@CYS (10 μg/mL) and incubation for 0.5 h. Upon the completion of the incubation period, the cells were washed thrice with PBS and imaged using a Zeiss LSM710 laser scanning confocal microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence in vivo imaging of Cys
ce:section.ce:para: Female BALB/c mice (6–8 weeks old) were provided by Hunan SJA Laboratory Animal Co., Ltd. (Changsha, China). All animal handling procedures were approved by the Animal Ethics Committee of Guangxi Normal University (Approval No. 202309-004). Ten mice were randomly divided into two groups. The first group received a subcutaneous injection of 100 μL of GCDs@CYS solution (20 μg/mL) and was subsequently placed in a small animal live imager for imaging. In the second group, NEM (100 μL, 10 mM) was first injected to remove biothiols in vivo, and a mouse model with low Cys levels was constructed. After 30 min, 100 μL of GCDs@CYS solution (20 μg/mL) was injected in situ, followed by imaging. The fluorescence of the GCDs and HCy-OH dye was measured using 700 and 750 nm filters, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and response mechanism of the GCDs@CYS probe for Cys detection
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent dye HCy-OH, which contains a carboxyl group, was first synthesized. Subsequently, the HCy-CYS organic probe was synthesized using acryloyl chloride and HCy-OH as raw materials. Fig. S1 shows the synthetic route for HCy-CYS. The relevant intermediates and final products of the synthesis of the probe were characterized using mass spectroscopy and nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy (Figs. S2–S14), which confirmed the successful synthesis of HCy-CYS. GCDs with a fluorescence emission at 685 nm were synthesized using GSH as the carbon source. The HCy-CYS probe was then covalently coupled to the GCDs using a coupling agent to form the GCDs@CYS nanocomposite probe. The GCDs served as the coupling agent in the GCDs@CYS probe, primarily functioning as the provider and donor of fluorescent signals and fluorescence energy transfer, respectively. The fluorescence of the CDs was quenched upon coupling with HCy-CYS, decreasing and increasing the fluorescence signal intensity at 720 and 685 nm, respectively. Consequently, the fluorescence of the HCy-CYS organic probe significantly weakened, whereas the GCDs exhibited strong fluorescence. In the presence of Cys, the acrylate on the probe reacts with Cys to form a seven-ring. The ester bond is then cleaved to form HCy-OH, which restores the fluorescence of the organic small-molecule probe. This activates the fluorescence resonance energy transfer.

(FRET) between HCy-OH and GCDs, weakening the fluorescence of the GCDs at 685 nm. Therefore, GCDs@CYS can be used as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for Cys detection by changing the reduction and enhancement of the fluorescence intensity at two wavelengths. Scheme 1 and Scheme 2 schematically illustrate the GCDs@CYS for Cys and the reaction mechanism between GCDs@CYS and Cys, respectively.

The surface morphology, degree of dispersion, and particle-size distribution of the GCDs were characterized using TEM. The TEM image in Fig. 1 a shows that the GCDs exhibit a dotted shape, uniform size, and good dispersion. The inset high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image shows the GCDs with a clear lattice stripe, indicating a lattice constant of 0.21 nm, which is consistent with the (100) crystalline surface of graphene, suggesting a high degree of graphitization. The GCD particle size distribution (Fig. 1b) indicates that they have an approximate diameter of 4.5 nm. XRD was used to evaluate the crystalline shape of the GCDs, revealing a broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 23.43° (Fig. S15) corresponding to the (002) amorphous carbon structure [33]. XPS was employed to investigate the elemental composition of the GCDs (Fig. 1c), which revealed that the GCDs are primarily composed of oxygen, carbon, and nitrogen, as evidenced by the strong O1s and C1s peaks at 532.7 and 285.1 eV, respectively, and the weak N1s peak at 399.1 eV.

The surface functional groups of the GCDs and GCDS@CYS were analyzed using FTIR spectrometry (Fig. 1d). A comparison of the FTIR spectra of GCDs@CYS and the GCDs revealed that the characteristic peak at 3410 cm−1 corresponds to the -O-H/N–H stretching vibration peak in GCDs@CYS, that at 2848 cm−1 corresponds to the C–H stretching vibration, that at 1634 cm−1 corresponds to the CC and CN bending vibrations, and that at 1360‒1020 cm−1 corresponds to the C–O and C–N bending vibrations. These results indicate that HCy@CYS was successfully modified on the GCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectral properties of the GCDs, HCy-OH, and GCDs@CYS
ce:section.ce:para: The absorption, fluorescence excitation, and emission spectra of the GCDs were measured (Fig. 2 a). As shown in the figure, the GCDs exhibit multiple absorption peaks at 400, 627, and 680 nm. Using 627 nm as the excitation wavelength resulted in the GCDs generating strong fluorescence emission at 685 nm. Subsequently, the absorption, fluorescence excitation, and emission spectra of the fluorescent dye HCy-OH were obtained (Fig. 2b). The absorption spectrum of HCy-OH exhibits a maximum absorption peak at 685 nm. Using 685 nm as the excitation wavelength caused HCy-OH to emit intense fluorescence at 720 nm. A comparison of Fig. 2a and b revealed that the fluorescence emission spectrum of the GCDs and the absorption spectrum of HCy-OH almost completely overlap (Fig. 2c), indicating that FRET can occur between them, which weakens the fluorescence of the GCDs and enhances the fluorescence of HCy-OH. The fluorescence spectra before and after the reaction of GCDs@CYS with Cys were subsequently examined (Fig. 2d). Fig. 2d shows that GCDs@CYS exhibits a strong fluorescence emission at 685 nm before reacting with Cys. The fluorescence emission intensity at 685 nm decreased after reacting with Cys. However, the increased fluorescence emission intensity at 720 nm suggests that HCy-OH was formed on the surface after the reaction of GCDs@CYS with Cys.

This study aimed to explore the feasibility of Cys detection using the GCDs@CYS probe. The fluorescence spectrum of the GCDs@CYS solution was obtained after adding a certain amount of Cys (Fig. 3 a). Excitation of the solution by 627 nmwavelength light prior to the addition of Cys resulted in the solution exhibiting the strongest fluorescence at 685 nm (emission peak of the GCDs), with no discernible fluorescence peak being observed at 720 nm. Excitation of the solution with a 627 nmwavelength light after the addition of excess Cys resulted in significant weakening of the solution fluorescence at 685 nm. Additionally, a new emission peak appeared at 720 nm. These results suggest that the ratiometric detection of Cys using the GCDs@CYS probe is feasible.

The fluorescence spectra of the GCDs@CYS mixed solution after the addition of different concentrations of Cys (0–200 μM) were analyzed under excitation at 627 nm (Fig. 3a). The fluorescence intensity of the solution at 685 nm decreased with a continuously increasing Cys concentration, whereas that at 720 nm increased. This trend demonstrates a clear correlation between the fluorescence response and Cys concentration. Additionally, the change in the fluorescence intensity ratio over time was evaluated to determine the in vivo imaging time (Fig. S16). The figure shows that the maximum ratio was reached at 10 min. Therefore, 10 min was selected as the reference time for in vivo imaging. The horizontal and vertical coordinates in Fig. 3b represent the concentration and ratio of the fluorescence intensity at 720 nm to that at 685 nm (F720 nm/F685 nm), respectively, for each concentration. A linear regression analysis yielded the equation: I = 0.00374C + 0.19095 (Fig. 3b). The plot shows that the fluorescence intensity ratio (F720 nm/F685 nm) of Cys exhibits a good linear relationship with the concentration in the range of 10–150 μM with a limit of detection of 0.95 μM (S/N = 3).

To assess the specificity of the GCDs@CYS probe, a series of tests were conducted under the same conditions using substances with similar structures, including GSH, Hcy, and hydrogen sulfide (H2S), as well as other amino acids and ions commonly encountered in vivo, including Met, tryptophan (Trp), glutamic acid (Glu), K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Ba2+, S2−, and NO2 −. The fluorescence spectra of the probe after 10-min reactions with these substances were examined, and the fluorescence intensity changes were observed. The results are shown in Fig. 3c and Fig. S17. The fluorescence intensity of the other components of the probe did not change significantly, except for a significant change in the fluorescence intensity after the reaction with Cys. Furthermore, GCDs@CYS exhibited excellent stability and was deemed suitable for in vivo imaging, as shown in Fig. S18‒S20. These findings suggest that GCDs@CYS possesses high specificity for Cys, enabling it to be distinguished from GSH and Hcy, which share similar structures. The experimental results align with the anticipated design of the HCy-CYS dye, which is intended to exhibit high selectivity for Cys. Consequently, the GCDs@CYS probe can be utilized for the precise in vivo detection of Cys.

The cytotoxicities of the GCDs and GCDs@CYS probe were assessed using the MTT assay (Fig. 3d). The survival rate of cells remained above 85 %, even at GCD and GCDs@CYS concentrations up to 200 μg/mL, indicating that GCDs@CYS exhibits good biocompatibility and low toxicity to cells; thus, they are suitable for in vivo imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cys content in cell lysate
ce:section.ce:para: HeLa cell lysates were used to investigate the ability of the GCDs@CYS probe to detect Cys in vivo. The results in Table 1 indicate that the Cys level in the cell lysates was approximately 30.45 μM. However, the recovery rate of Cys was 98.4%–102.16 % with a relative standard deviation of <3.4 %. Therefore, the GCDs@CYS probe is feasible for detecting Cys levels in real samples such as cell lysates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratio fluorescence imaging of Cys in HeLa cells
ce:section.ce:para: HeLa cells were used as imaging objects to evaluate the imaging capabilities of the GCDs@CYS probe in cells. The ability of the probe to visualize and analyze the endogenous Cys levels in HeLa cells was also investigated, as shown in Fig. 4 . In Group (a), HeLa cells were co-incubated with GCDs@CYS for 30 min, with fluorescence being observed in both the 685 and 720 nm channels under 633 nm light excitation. In Group (b), NEM was added and incubated for 30 min to remove the thiols. GCDs@CYS was then added and co-incubated with HeLa cells for 30 min. Post-imaging analyses revealed strong fluorescence emission in the 685 nm channel, whereas minimal fluorescence was observed at 720 nm. In Group (c), the cells were incubated with NEM for 30 min, incubated with Cys solution for 30 min, and finally the HeLa cells were incubated with GCDs@CYS for 30 min. The fluorescence in the 685 nm channel decreased, whereas that in the 720 nm channel increased. These results show that the GCDs@CYS probe can detect changes in the intracellular Cys levels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo ratio imaging of Cys
ce:section.ce:para: This study investigated the ability of the composite probe GCDs@CYS to detect Cys in vivo using its proven imaging ability in cells. The BALB/c mice were divided into two groups. The first group was injected subcutaneously with the GCDs@CYS probe solution after anesthesia and subsequently placed in a small animal in vivo imager for imaging. Fig. 5 a and b shows that the fluorescence intensity of the probe in the 700 nm filter channel decreased over time, while that in the 750 nm filter channel increased. Excess NEM was injected in the second group to scavenge biothiols and construct a low-Cys level mouse model. After 30 min, the GCDs@CYS probe solution was injected in situ and imaged (Fig. 5c and d). The figure indicates that the GCDs@CYS probe exhibits good imaging and analysis capabilities for Cys in vivo. The probe can dynamically trace changes in Cys levels in real-time, providing imaging information for disease development and offering a new method for diagnosis and treatment.

A novel ratiometric fluorescent probe with dual near-infrared emission (GCDs@CYS) was prepared in this study for the in vivo ratio imaging of Cys. The probe specifically distinguishes Cys from GSH and Hcy. The GCDs in the GCDs@CYS probe served as both carriers and energy donors for the organic small-molecule probe. The GCDs entered cells through endocytosis, effectively avoiding the limitations of single organic small-molecule probes such as poor water solubility, susceptibility to removal, and photobleaching. Furthermore, the GCDs@CYS probe exhibited both excitation and emission wavelengths in the near-infrared region. This characteristic renders it highly suitable for practical applications such as the real-time imaging and detection of Cys in vivo. The in vitro and in vivo experimental results showed that the designed and synthesized GCDs@CYS probes can be used to perform dynamic and precise imaging analyses of exogenous and endogenous Cys levels in a ratiometric manner. This strategy is expected to provide a new reference for the detection and diagnosis of Cys-related diseases.

Bing Zheng: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Shulong Wang: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jiayao Xu: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Lixian Huang: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Shulin Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Data curation, Conceptualization.

All animal experiments were performed in accordance with the guidelines of the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (IACUC) and were approved by the Laboratory Animal Care and Animal Ethics Committee of Guangxi Normal University.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundations of China (No. 22364021), Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Province (2024GXNSFAA010250, 2024GXNSFBA010251), as well as BAGUI Scholar Program.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2025.127564.
